strategy: agentic chunking via Gemini (headline + summary + original text)

pages.text:
2

Now that you've learned all the information you need to create individualized nutritional
programs, in this week we'll go over some case studies to see how it all fits together.
When creating nutrition programs, it generally helps to go by the following order.
1. Determine the meal frequency (3-4 generally) and times, as well as sleep-wake
times (ideally in line with daylight with a consistent 7.5+ hours of sleep) and
workout times (ideally afternoon to early evening). Ensure someone isn't training
fasted, the workouts are adequately surrounded by meals and there are enough
meals in the anabolic window to fuel maximum MPS.
2. Determine if any meals fall largely outside the anabolic window (no significant
elevation of MPS from baseline anymore). Note that for simplicity's sake, you
could also skip this step and simply allocate the same macros to all days, but it
may not be optimal for nutrient partitioning.
3. Allocate the minimum feasible energy intake to those meals: generally 8.3-9.7
kcal/g protein if you want to do a proper PSMF.
4. Use the Henselmans PT Course Energy Intake Calculator to calculate the weekly
total energy intake.
5. Distribute the remaining calories to your remaining meals. Tweak the energy
intake per meal to synchronize with the anabolic window: see the next step. You
can generally distribute protein and energy intake in the same ratio over time with
50-100% more energy and protein intake in meals squarely in the anabolic
window (maximum MPS) compared to meals outside of it.
6. Set the daily average protein intake (minimum 1.6 g/kg/d) and distribute this in
line with MPS during the anabolic windows. As a rule of thumb, allocate 50-100%
more protein to meals in the peak of the anabolic window compared to meals
outside it. For example, if someone eats 3 meals a day, one pre-workout and 2
post-workout, and you want 50% more protein intake in meals 2 and 3 compared
to meal 1, then you divide the total daily protein intake by 1 + 1.5 + 1.5 = 4 and
you allocate that number to meal 1 and 1.5x that number to meals 2 and 3.
If you have 4 meals and you want meals 3 and 4 to be double the size of meals 1

3

and 2, you divide total daily protein intake by 1 + 1 + 2 + 2 = 6 and you allocate
that to meals 1 and 2 and 2x that to meals 3 and 4.
7. Set the average daily fat intake and divide that equally across meals. If you use
PSMF periods, it's often convenient not to set any fat intake target for those
meals and err on the side of a higher fat intake (up to 40% for non-ketogenic
diets) for meals in the anabolic window.
Alternatively, you can also simply multiply all meal energy intake by the target fat
intake to get the target fat intake per meal. Note that the distribution of fat intake
over time doesn't matter per se, but the distribution of energy intake over time
most likely does.
8. Set carbohydrate intake for each meal by difference: take the meal's energy
intake and subtract 4x protein intake and 9x fat intake and then divide by 4 to get
the meal's carbohydrate intake target. If needed, tweak the carb and fat intakes
in each meal to implement carb backloading.
9. Check that the diet allows for a sustainable and enjoyable meal plan. Simplify it
as much as possible without compromising its effectiveness and set error
margins or guidelines to clarify the allowed deviations. For example, you can
generally set protein, fat and fiber intakes as minima rather than set targets.

4

The steps to creating an optimal nutrition program.

The above is useful as a standard approach, but you'll see some of the case studies
use a more flexible case-by-case method depending on the client. The best way to learn
is to create many programs yourself. The first programs may take hours, but over time
you'll develop your own methods and you'll get much quicker at it. Let's get into some
examples, but first, there are a few points that warrant emphasizing for the case studies.
1. The goal of this course is not to learn to blindly copy Menno's methods. It is to
understand the principles of evidence-based nutrition and training program
design. Some of these case studies are from clients Menno had years ago and
Determine daily schedule with meal, training and sleep times
If some meals/days fall outside the anabolic window, consider a PSMF for these to allocate all
reamaining kcal to the anabolic windows
Use the Henselmans PT Course Energy Intake Calculator to calculate the weekly total energy
intake
Distribute remaining kcal to anabolic window meals or training days
Calculate daily average protein intake and distribute this in line with anabolic windows (50-
100% more post-workout)
Set % fat intake and distribute equally across non-PSMF periods
Calculate carbohydrate intake per meal by remaining kcal
Do a practicality check of the nutrition plan

5

now he might estimate their body fat percentage, ideal fat intake, TEF or optimal
energy balance factor slightly differently. Applied personal training is where
science meets art: you will have to make many judgment calls which do not have
'one correct answer'.
2. In line with the above, the idea of these case studies is to give you an illustration
of how a reasonable program for the given individual looks like. You'll get the
best educational results if you try to design a program yourself based on the
course principles and then see if it's similar to the case study's program. If it's
not, that's what the group discussion is for. Some people may prefer to 'just get
the answer', but that's not very educational. It's the same as in math class:
everything looks easy when you see the answer in front of you, but only by doing
the calculations yourself do you achieve true insight into the process so you learn
to do it yourself.
3. Let go of the constrained thinking of diets as being set up for calendar days.
Instead, start thinking in terms of anabolic window periods over time for an
individual. Outside of the anabolic window, PSMF periods are arguably the most
efficient use of someone's time, since they maximize fat loss and free up more
calories to be allocated to the anabolic window periods where nutrient partitioning
is better and they can contribute to muscle growth rather than fat gain. On the
other hand, you may want to simplify your programs for the sake of convenience
and diet adherence. You'll see examples of both very sophisticated and much
more simplified approaches in the case studies.

Lastly, if you're confident in your grasp on the materials, you don't need to go through
all the case studies for the exam. There are no new materials in the case studies, just
examples of applications of the course principles. Essentially, this whole module is
optional.

• Case study: PSMF

6

• Case study: PSMF 2
This case study was created by a PT Course student. It details the calculation
steps well.
• Case study: simplified anabolic window synching
• Case study: female Powerlifter in contest prep
This is a case study of a PT Course student setting up her diet for her upcoming
Powerlifting contest. You can see the feedback from the course group and me
along with her subsequent changes.
• Case study John: untrained male on a ketogenic diet
• Case study: Ketogenic diet 2
This case study was created by a PT Course student for one of his clients after
feedback from the course group.
• Case study Marco: intermediate+ bulk
• Case study Matthew: novice male
• Case study Kevin: intermediate male
• Case study heavyweight bodybuilder in off-season: This one is in spreadsheet
format so you can see the exact calculations and corresponding comments.
• Sample program Bart: Here I had to create a diet plan that he could follow on his
own, so you can see how I started simple and incorporated more nutrient timing
in the second phase of his diet
• Case study Chad: intermediate-advanced high level BJJ competitor
